steps.title: Identify the claim and its foundation
steps.content: The archaeologist claims the notches were "made at one time by one individual as a means of counting something." This claim rests on the pattern — unevenly spaced, approximately parallel notches made with a single tool — being distinctive of numerical recording. To weaken the claim, a finding must show that this pattern does not uniquely indicate counting, i.e., it appears in non-numerical contexts too.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — "Parallel lines are a common feature in modern humans' early systems for recording numerical information.": actually supports the claim by linking parallel lines to counting systems. ✓ for the wrong reason — this strengthens, not weakens. ✗
  • Choice B — "More than nine approximately parallel notches made with a different stone tool are present on another artifact found at a site in western France.": a different tool on a different artifact tells us nothing about whether either artifact was used for counting. ✗
  • Choice C — "It would have taken careful effort to make evenly spaced lines on bone with the stone tools typically used by Neanderthals.": addresses effort to make evenly spaced lines, not the significance of uneven spacing on this bone — irrelevant to the counting claim. ✗
  • Choice D — "Decorative art discovered at another Neanderthal site in western France primarily features patterns of unevenly spaced parallel lines.": shows that the same pattern — unevenly spaced parallel lines — appears in purely decorative contexts, meaning the pattern does not uniquely signal counting and the bone could be decorative rather than numerical. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
